EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Drought Response Committee declared many parts of the state in a moderate drought.  For the year, the state received well below the normal rainfall...averaging 8-9 inches below normal.  The below normal rainfall actually began in 1999, and since that time the Pee Dee and the Grand Strand area are about 20 inches below normal.  The main result of the lack of precipitation was above normal wildfires...scorching 2500 acres in November alone, verses 950 acres on average.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system caused heavy showers and wind gusts 30 to 50 mph that downed a few powerlines...but widespread rainwater flooding along the Grand Strand caused more serious problems. Radar rainfall estimates and local reports indicated 2-4 inches of rain in a couple of hours, and 6 inches to 2 feet of water flooded many roads and intersections. Police reported 30 roads were barricaded. A 7 year old boy drowned when he ventured into a flooded ditch along Sycamore Street near the entrance of Sea Gate Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic high pressure system built over the deep South at mid month, producing not only record cold temperatures for this time of year, but also contributing to records for late season snowfall at some locations as a potent upper level disturbance affected the region later in the day. With a tight pressure gradient in place, winds gusted as high as 55 mph at some foothill and piedmont locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extremely late-season snowfall left a dusting of snow as far east as the South Carolina piedmont, despite surface temperatures in the middle 40s.

A strong Arctic high pressure system built over the deep South at mid month, producing not only record cold temperatures for this time of year, but also contributing to records for late season snowfall at some locations as a potent upper level disturbance affected the region later in the day. With a tight pressure gradient in place, winds gusted as high as 55 mph at some foothill and piedmont locations.

Low pressure developed off the South Carolina coast and steadily strengthened as it moved northward across the coastal waters of North Carolina, the Virginia tidewater and eventually out to sea. Rapid strengthening occurred as a strong upper level disturbance rotated around an upper low that was crossing the southeast states. As the cyclone strengthened, abundant moisture was wrapped around the storm and thrown back against the higher terrain of the Carolinas, resulting in high winds and snow, which was heavy in a few locations.The highest snowfall report from western South Carolina occurred in the watershed area of northern Greenville County, near Table Rock, where 8 to 12 inches of snow fell. Caesars Head reported 6 inches of snow, and the Cliffs of Glassy Mountain checked in with 5 inches, Most of the other significant accumulations in western South Carolina were confined to the mountainous regions bordered by Highway 11, with 4 to 6 inch accumulations reported from northern Pickens and northern Oconee counties. The town of Salem checked in with 5 inches. Outside of the mountains, Landrum, in northwest Spartanburg County, led all reporting stations with 5 inches...with isolated 4 inch amounts from southern Greenville County.Winds were estimated at between 40 and 50 mph at most upstate locations during the height of the storm, although gusts higher than that occurred in the higher terrain. Many thousands of trees were downed in the mountains, while the number of downed trees in the foothills and piedmont was more scattered in nature. The character of the wind in the mountains was a steady 35 to 45 mph wind of long duration, with gusts to near 60. However, damage was most significant in the foothills and piedmont, despite slightly lower speeds estimated there. An outbuilding in northern Abbeville County was destroyed, along with most of the contents. The porch and roof of the house on the property were also damaged. A dock was torn from a pier at Hartwell Lake, setting boats adrift. 100 trees were downed in Easley. In Spartanburg county, a roof was blown off a mobile home, and a county sheriff car was blown off the road at Pauline. An awning was torn of a storefront in Anderson County. Some trees fell on structures and vehicles throughout the upstate region.

The character of the wind in the mountains was a steady 35 to 45 mph wind of long duration, with gusts to near 60. However, damage was most significant in the foothills and piedmont, despite slightly lower speeds estimated there. An outbuilding in northern Abbeville County was destroyed, along with most of the contents. The porch and roof of the house on the property were also damaged. A dock was torn from a pier at Hartwell Lake, setting boats adrift. 100 trees were downed in Easley. In Spartanburg county, a roof was blown off a mobile home, and a county sheriff car was blown off the road at Pauline. An awning was torn of a storefront in Anderson County. Some trees fell on structures and vehicles throughout the upstate region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the South Carolina coast and steadily strengthened as it moved northward across the coastal waters of North Carolina, the Virginia tidewater and eventually out to sea. Rapid strengthening occurred as a strong upper level disturbance rotated around an upper low that was crossing the southeast states. As the cyclone strengthened, abundant moisture was wrapped around the storm and thrown back against the higher terrain of the Carolinas, resulting in high winds and snow, which was heavy in a few locations.The highest snowfall report from western South Carolina occurred in the watershed area of northern Greenville County, near Table Rock, where 8 to 12 inches of snow fell. Caesars Head reported 6 inches of snow, and the Cliffs of Glassy Mountain checked in with 5 inches, Most of the other significant accumulations in western South Carolina were confined to the mountainous regions bordered by Highway 11, with 4 to 6 inch accumulations reported from northern Pickens and northern Oconee counties. The town of Salem checked in with 5 inches. Outside of the mountains, Landrum, in northwest Spartanburg County, led all reporting stations with 5 inches...with isolated 4 inch amounts from southern Greenville County.Winds were estimated at between 40 and 50 mph at most upstate locations during the height of the storm, although gusts higher than that occurred in the higher terrain. Many thousands of trees were downed in the mountains, while the number of downed trees in the foothills and piedmont was more scattered in nature. The character of the wind in the mountains was a steady 35 to 45 mph wind of long duration, with gusts to near 60. However, damage was most significant in the foothills and piedmont, despite slightly lower speeds estimated there. An outbuilding in northern Abbeville County was destroyed, along with most of the contents. The porch and roof of the house on the property were also damaged. A dock was torn from a pier at Hartwell Lake, setting boats adrift. 100 trees were downed in Easley. In Spartanburg county, a roof was blown off a mobile home, and a county sheriff car was blown off the road at Pauline. An awning was torn of a storefront in Anderson County. Some trees fell on structures and vehicles throughout the upstate region.

As a weak cold front moved south into an afternoon sea breeze boundary, slow-moving thunderstorms developed along the Horry county coast. An F0 tornado briefly touched down at 9th Ave N near the Myrtle Beach Pavilion, and soon after a waterspout formed just off the beach near 3rd Ave N. It slightly damaged the 2nd Ave N pier and then moved over the beach as it developed more strongly, causing F2 damage - overturned buses and extensive damage to vehicles and hotels along the beach to about 4nd Ave S. Moving off the beach again, the waterspout continued south about 100 yards from the shore until it came ashore between 28th Ave S and Springmaid Pier...causing a 66 knot gust over water at the Springmaid Pier anemometer. As it moved through the Seagate RV park, it did F1 damage - destroyed 10 RVs and damaged 40 more. Weakening further, the tornado crossed US Hwy Business 17 onto Myrtle Beach International Airport, doing F0 damage to trees and structures.
Lightning caused a house fire on Gray Man Loop in Waccamaw Neck.
Longs fire department reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month began with moderate drought conditions which continued from the past couple of months. After several soaking rains, drought conditions lessened to become mild during the last half of the month.

A ridge of high pressure had built over the southeast states late in June. Meanwhile, tropical air was trapped under the ridge axis across the southeast states. Flow through the mid levels of the atmosphere was almost non-existent, resulting in numerous slow-moving showers and thunderstorms, that produced torrential rainfall in areas they traversed. Flooding events on the 25th were all produced by deep convection that stayed in the same area until the cells had rained themselves out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little River flooded within the city limits. Numerous low spots were blocked by standing water. Cars stalled in flood water on US 76.

Trained spotter report of 55 to 60 mph winds.

State highway 146 was flooded, with at least three feet of water over the roadway at one time just a few miles southeast of Woodruff. Numerous other roads in the area were also closed because of flooding. An officer lost his patrol car in the flood waters as he attempted to maneuver through the high water. A wrecker was unable to attempt a rescue of the vehicle because it couldn't get through the flood water. Radar estimated over 10 inches of rain in the flood area.

Several eyewitnesses reported a tornado touching down during a thunderstorm that was effectively stationary for at least 15 minutes. Path of the tornado was mostly through the woods. Fire departmentfound 2 large oak trees with broken limbs. NWS storm survey team received 5 images of the tornado. The tornado was a well-defined vortex descending from an apparent small wall cloud. Radar presentation displayed a small thunderstorm, or moderate to heavy rain shower, with only a 30,000 foot top.
Large tree limbs down.
Lightning sparked a fire which destroyed an unoccupied, wooden frame house.
Lightning blew a transformer at a manufacturing plant, shutting it down for more than 24 hours and keeping 1000 employees off the job.
Lightning caused a couple of house fires, which heavily damaged the houses.

Numerous trees and power lines blown down. Traffic lights were blown to the ground. One tree tore through the roof of a house, and several large limbs fell through roofs. Damage track from north of city to Lake Greenwood. Highway Patrol also reported debris from a trailer blown across a road. A tin room was blown off a building at Cedar Springs Golf Course, where 20 to 25 trees were downed and a 65-pound battery charger was blown onto the roof of the clubhouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in Anderson County as a strong cold front crossed western South Carolina on the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines brought down by the wind. Widespread power outages. Some trees fell onto cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture overspread the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia as the remnants of a tropical wave moved from the Gulf Coast across the southeast states. Numerous cooperative observer stations in the region reported over 3 inches of rainfall from late morning on the 25th through the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams and creeks flooded and roads were closed in, and around, the city. A number of trees were downed by the high water and wet ground.
Sheriff reported trees and powerlines down in the Lexington area.
Long Creek-Grady fire department reported nickel size hail.
Emergency manager reported trees and power lines down on West Palmetto St.
Several trees down.

Numerous trees and power lines down around Keowee Key, and as far west as West Union. One person was trapped in a car by a fallen tree. At least 100 trees were knocked down onto roads, and another 100 on private property.
A trained spotter measured a wind gust to 58 mph. Lightning struck a church in the Cheddar community, between Williamston and Belton. The resulting fire destroyed the main building.
Bypass flooded in low spots during slow moving thunderstorms. No known stream flooding.
Small streams and roads flooded during heavy rain.
Small streams and creeks overflowed onto roads from the Pickens county line, down to the city of Anderson. Some vehicles were partially submerged, and a pond overflowed and flooded SC 86.

Several roads closed due to flooding. Gilder Creek in Simpsonville was out of its banks in several locations. Urban, street and small stream flooding started in Greenville around noon. A car was partially submerged on the Greenville County side of Piedmont, and a teenager tubing Gilder Creek in Mauldin was swept downstream some distance, but later rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dying squall line moving out of North Carolina held together long enough to produce a short-lived but extensive swath of downed trees and power lines in the northern counties of upstate South Carolina.

Heavy rains caused flooding at many intersections as well as the lower end or the causeway to the island.

Many trees blown down, some on cars and onto property.
Sheriff reported a few trees down in the Whitehall area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system meandered slowly across the southeast states during the latter half of Labor Day weekend, interacting with moisture associated with a weakening tropical disturbance as it streamed northward into the Carolinas. The heavy rain that resulted in upstate South Carolina alleviated, albeit briefly, short term drought concerns. However, by mid September, dry weather returned to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gilder Creek flooded at the lower end, flooding parts of Kingswood Estates subdivision following 4 inches of rain that fell in just two hours between Five Forks and Clear Spring.
Mauldin Police Department reported numerous trees, and all city flags flying downtown, blown down. Dime-sized hail also fell.
Brief damaging winds occurred during a fast-moving, low-topped line of thunderstorms.
Urban flooding in and around Spartanburg and Inman during slow-moving afternoon thunderstorms.

At least one person required rescue at the intersection of Highway 291 and Interstate 85, following persistent heavy rain. Many roads flooded in southern Greenville County following a storm total rainfall of 4 to 7 inches.
Spotter reported quarter size hail.
One large tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The effects of the long-term drought became more severe. Although the region did receive some rain during August, the state of South Carolina did record its fifth-driest month ever.

A squall line developed in north central Georgia, ahead of a strong cold front, and pushed across Upstate South Carolina late in the afternoon of the 31st. The thunderstorms were fast moving, at speeds of up to 60 mph. Very dry air aloft contributed to the widespread reports of wind damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines down in both towns.
A large tree was blown across cars at the police station motor pool, causing a significant amount of damage to employees' cars.
Two trees blown down.
Trees and power lines blown down. Hail the size of quarters was also observed. Two of the downed trees fell on homes.
Numerous trees and power lines blown down from Union to the Chester County line.
Numerous trees and power lines brought down in Greenville. Over 3000 homes were without power after the storm.
Large tree limbs blown down in Simpsonville.
Trees and power lines blwon down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the center of Tropical Storm Gabrielle passed 200 miles off the coast, gale-force winds and 11 foot seas over the coastal waters combined with high tides to cause coastal flooding in Garden City. The fire department reported that Atlantic and Cypress Avenues were underwater and impassable. Minor flooding due to the rising tides was also reported at Caswell Beach and Oak Island.
Trees and power lines blown down halfway between Chester and Rock Hill.
A couple of trees and some power lines blown down from Smyrna to York.
Large tree limbs blown across power lines in Rock Hill, resulting in power outages.

Trees and power lines blown down.
A metal shed was blown across Highway 11.
A 38 year old man was struck and killed by lightning as he huddled near a beach umbrella that his family was under.

Trees blown down, and bark ripped off of trees, at Lake Lyman. Some power lines and power outages resulted. Also, an off-duty NWS employee in Campobello reported marble-sized hail and estimated winds of 55 to 60 mph.
Trees and power lines blown over from western Pickens County, to just east of the city of Pickens. 3000 homes were without power after the storm.
Numerous trees and power lines blown down, taking out power for hours across all of Dacusville and parts of Easley.
Numerous trees and power lines blown down in West Pelzer.

Outflow from a severe thunderstorm, centered just across the border in northeast Laurens County near Whitmire, caused some telephone poles to be snapped. Also, railroad-crossing arms were bent, and damage was reported at a lumber yard.
Corp of Engineers reported trees down on a home on the south shore of Clarks Hill Lake.
Georgetown 911 reported a person was struck by lightning.  The extent of the injuries is unknown. A structure was also reported to be on fire due to lightning.  The damage amount is unknown.
Public reported quarter size hail.
Lightning struck a church on 4029 Hill Street.  There was damage to the roof near the steeple, along with some other exterior damage.
Emergency manager reported that two people were struck by lightning.  The extent of the injuries is unknown.
Lightning struck an over 100 year old home on 430 Highmarket St. , severely damaging it.
Public reported dime size hail.
Public reported dime size hail.
Trees and power lines blown onto roads in extreme southern Cherokee County.
Large tree limbs down along Highway 61.
Large limbs and power lines down.
Fallen limbs from thunderstorm winds fell on power lines, causing power outages. A split tree blocked a street for some time. Rainfall of 1.5 inches was also recorded.
Trees, large limbs and power lines down.
Lightning caused fire burned down  a 7500 square foot home.

A 32 year old man experienced a double jolt from lightening within a 10 minute span. The man was driving a Toyota pickup when a bolt of lightning struck his CB antenna. This caused the antenna to pop off, it broke out the rear window on the cab and blew out the left rear tire. When he stopped the truck to get out to assess the damage, a second bolt of lightning hit the bed of the pickup and the force threw him out into the roadway.
Waterspout sited off Kiawah Island moved northeast and then dissipated.
A few large limbs were blown down.
State trooper reported trees down along U.S. 221.
Trees blown over onto U.S. 25.
Lightning resulted in numerous power outages.
Several trees and power lines down.
Sheriff reported a few trees down in the St. Andrews area.
Sheriff and Electric Co. reported trees and powerlines down in southeast fairfield county.
Large limbs and power lines down.

Wind damage occurred at the construction site of Dorman High School, at the intersection of Interstate 26 and U.S. 221. A construction trailer was damaged, debris scattered and a 1000-pound roof vent was removed and rolled a short distance. Trees were felled in the Roebuck area of Spartanburg County at about the same time.
Numerous large trees were blown down across roads. County officials reported that Public Works crews were out all night cleaning up.
General public report of a few trees down just south of the Spartanburg County line.
Numerous trees, limbs, and power lines down countywide.
Trees and power lines down in numerous locations. Two printing plants lost power for several hours as a result of lightning hitting transformers nearby.
A tree fell on a moving car. At press time, no confirmation of injuries.
Lightning struck a garage business, destroying the building and a motorcycle located inside.
Horry Skywarn reported rainwater flooding 1 foot deep at Ocean Blvd and 2nd Ave N, which was closed by police. Radar estimated 4 to 5 inches fell over a 2.5 hour period.
Emergency manager reported flooding on Church and Dargan Street.  A spotter also reported flooding on 2nd Loop Rd. and other residential streets in the area.
Emergency manager reported street flooding in Florence on West Palmetto St., Church St., Irby St., and Dargan St.
Post Office reported trees down on powerlines south of Cope.

Drought was again the major concern during the month. An extended short-term dry spell exacerbated the ongoing drought, lasting from mid-October through much of November. During this stretch of weeks, many areas received no measurable rain.Toward the end of the month, a few wildfires were noted across the mountains, burning many acres. Extremely low lake levels affected boating and water supplies. Many additional wells and ponds dried up, tree farms closed and many communities enacted or expanded water restrictions. Record low streamflows were observed on area streams and rivers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in extreme northern Pickens County as a squall line moved in from the west. Some trees fell onto roads. The first area affected was north of Highway 11, west of Table Rock.
Police reported dime-sized hail fell in Greer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sporadic wind damage, and large hail, developed ahead of, and along with, a squall line that moved out of north Georgia early on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown over onto Highway 55.
